walkthrough.title: Place the Reputation Board
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Above and Below: Haunted set up. Find the large reputation board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This board has the reputation track running down the left side, spaces for villager cards across the top, and rows for house and outpost cards along the bottom.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Building Display
walkthrough.text: Now look at the building cards. You've got three types to sort out. Find the 6 star house cards, the ones with a star symbol, and lay them face up in a row above the reputation board. Shuffle the 9 key house cards, deal 4 face up below the star houses, and box the rest. Then shuffle the regular house deck and the outpost deck separately, and deal 4 face up from each below the key houses.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Cave Decks
walkthrough.text: Find the cave cards. You'll notice there are two types: normal caves and dangerous caves. The dangerous ones have a distinct border so you can tell them apart. Shuffle each type into its own face-down deck and place them near the reputation board. Dangerous caves are tougher to explore but give bigger rewards, including pearls.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Villager Display
walkthrough.text: Set aside any special villagers face up nearby, these are the unique characters you can earn through encounters. Shuffle the remaining regular villagers face down into a pile. Draw 5 villagers and place them on the reputation board's villager display slots. The slots have different costs, with cheaper ones at the bottom.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Tokens and Supplies
walkthrough.text: Sort the goods tokens by type and place them in accessible piles: fruit, fish, mushroom, rope, clay pot, paper, ore, and amethyst. Put the coins nearby. Place the ghost tokens, boat tokens, pearl tokens, potion tokens, and cider tokens in separate piles within reach. Set the encounter book to one side and grab the 7 dice.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the deck of 40 adventure cards. Give them a good shuffle and place them face down near the cave decks. You won't draw from this deck directly. Instead, adventure cards are gained as specific rewards when you succeed at cave encounters. They provide special abilities, immediate bonuses, or end-game victory points.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reputation Track
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes the matching wooden cube. Place all cubes on the torch space of the reputation track on the reputation board. This track goes from surface level down into the caves. Moving down means gaining reputation, moving up means losing it. Place 1 cider token on the cider slot of the reputation board.

walkthrough.title: Deal Player Boards and Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board and places it in front of them. You'll see distinct areas on it: the ready area on the left, the exhausted area, the injured area, and the advancement track along the bottom. Each player also gets a starting house card, which goes next to their player board. Give each player 7 coins.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Villagers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their 3 starting villagers and places them in the ready area of their player board. These villagers have a house symbol on the back. In a 4-player game, 2 of your starting villagers without hammer symbols start in the exhausted area instead and won't be ready until after round 1. Check each villager's icons. Hammers let you build, quills let you recruit or banish.

walkthrough.title: Handle Starting Bonuses and First Player
walkthrough.text: In games with more than 2 players, later players get bonus starting coins to offset the first-player advantage. Check the rules for your player count. The second and third players each get 1 extra coin in a 3-player game. Choose a random starting player and give them the first-player card. Place the round marker on the first position.

walkthrough.title: Setup Checkpoint
walkthrough.text: Let's do a quick check. The center of the table should have the reputation board with star houses, key houses, regular houses, and outposts displayed in rows. Two cave decks should be nearby, plus the adventure card deck. Each player should have a player board with a starting house, 3 villagers in the ready area, 7 coins, and a colored cube on the reputation track. Good to go!

walkthrough.title: The Seven Rounds
walkthrough.text: Above and Below: Haunted is played over exactly 7 rounds. There's also an optional longer variant that extends to 8 rounds, but we'll stick with the standard game for now. Each round, players take turns clockwise. On your turn, you'll assign villagers from your ready area to perform one main action. When you've used all your villagers or don't want to act anymore, you pass. Once everyone passes, the round ends.

walkthrough.title: Free Actions Before Your Turn
walkthrough.text: Before you take your main action each turn, you can do any number of free actions. You can place a good for sale in your sell area on your player board. You can buy a good from another player for at least 3 coins. You can pay 1 coin to refresh the house or outpost display by discarding all 4 face-up cards and drawing 4 new ones. And here's a new one: you can spend a boat token to either auto-roll a six on one of your dice or gain 1 coin. These are all free, they don't use up your main action.

walkthrough.title: Action: Explore
walkthrough.text: Exploring is the heart of the game. You need at least 2 villagers to explore. Draw the top cave card from either the normal or dangerous cave deck. Slide your villagers from the ready area onto the cave card. Then roll 1 die and check the cave card's grid to find which encounter paragraph to read from the encounter book. The player to your left reads it aloud, including the choices and their explore numbers, but not the rewards in parentheses.

walkthrough.title: Resolving an Exploration
walkthrough.text: After hearing your options, pick one choice aloud. Now roll 1 die for each villager on the cave card. Each villager token shows what rolls produce lanterns. For example, a villager might give 1 lantern on a roll of 1 or higher, and 2 lanterns on a 3 or higher. Total up all your lanterns. Quick heads up, if you're short on lanterns, you can exert villagers. That means moving them to the injured area instead of exhausted, and you get 1 extra lantern per exerted villager.

walkthrough.title: Exploration Results and New Rewards
walkthrough.text: If your lantern total meets or exceeds the explore number for your chosen option, you succeed and gain the rewards. Beat the number by 2 or more and you often get bonus rewards on top. If you explored a dangerous cave and succeeded, you also get a pearl. Even if you fail, you still keep the cave card, which is different from the original game. Remaining villagers on the cave card move to your exhausted area. Adventure cards are sometimes given as specific encounter rewards.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build
walkthrough.text: To build, you need a villager with a hammer icon. Slide that villager from ready to exhausted and pay the coin cost shown on the building card you want. Houses, key houses, and star houses go in a row next to your starting house above ground. Outposts are different. They must be placed on an empty cave card you own, representing underground construction. After you build, draw a replacement card so there are always 4 face up in each display row. Villagers with a hammer-plus symbol pay 1 fewer coin.

walkthrough.title: Action: Harvest
walkthrough.text: This action combines harvesting and laboring into one flexible move. Slide one or more villagers from ready to exhausted. For each villager you exhaust, you can take 1 goods token from one of your buildings or gain 1 coin. You can mix and match across your villagers. The first player to harvest each round also picks up the cider token from the reputation board, which is handy for refreshing villagers later.

walkthrough.title: Action: Recruit
walkthrough.text: To recruit, you need a villager with a quill icon. Slide the quill villager from ready to exhausted and pay the coin cost shown for any available villager on the reputation board. Your new hire goes to your exhausted area, ready to work next round. Quick heads up, here's the ghost catch: if you recruit from the 3 cheapest slots, you must place a ghost token on one of your buildings. Those villagers are coming through the haunted marsh to reach your village. Quill-plus villagers pay 1 fewer coin.

walkthrough.title: Action: Banish
walkthrough.text: This is a brand new action for Haunted. You need a quill villager and at least one ghost on your buildings. Exhaust the quill villager to remove ALL ghost tokens from your buildings. Then take one of those removed ghosts and place it on any opponent's building that doesn't already have a ghost. You gain a reward based on what type of building you targeted. The remaining removed ghosts go back to the supply. It's a satisfying move: clean your village and haunt someone else's.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Ghosts
walkthrough.text: Ghosts are the signature mechanic of this version. Wooden ghost tokens sit on your building cards when they get haunted. At the end of each round, ghosts on your buildings push your reputation down, which is bad. Ghosts show up when you recruit cheap villagers, from certain encounter results, and when opponents banish ghosts onto you. Each building can only hold one ghost. Managing ghosts is a constant balancing act. Sometimes tolerating a ghost or two is worth it if you're focused on other priorities.

walkthrough.title: Boats, Pearls, and Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: Three more new things to know about. Boat tokens are gained through encounters and building effects. Spend one as a free action to auto-roll a 6 on one die or gain 1 coin. Pearls come from successful dangerous cave explorations and count as your highest-value good on the advancement track at endgame. Adventure cards are the 40 unique cards gained from specific encounter rewards. They can give you immediate benefits, ongoing powers, or end-game victory points. Think of them as bonus loot from your cave adventures.

walkthrough.title: The Advancement Track and Income
walkthrough.text: Look at the bottom of your player board. That's your advancement track, a row of circles where you place goods. Fill them left to right, no gaps. If you get a second good of the same type, it stacks on the same slot. Each slot shows two numbers: the left number is how many VP each good in that slot is worth at game end, and the right number is your current income. More variety in goods means more income each round and higher VP per good.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: When all players have passed, the round ends. First, spend potions to move injured villagers to exhausted. Spend cider to move exhausted villagers to ready. Then use beds on your buildings, each bed symbol moves one villager one step. Next, check for ghost penalties on the reputation track. Collect income based on your advancement track position. Slide remaining villagers left on the reputation board and refill empty slots. Replenish the cider token. Pass the first-player card clockwise. Advance the round marker.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: After 7 rounds, it's time to score. Add up VP from your advancement track goods, pearl bonuses, building ownership at 1 VP each, reputation track bonuses, star building conditions, and adventure card points. Most reputation earns 5 VP, second most earns 3, third earns 2. In a 2-player game, only the leader gets 3 VP. Coins don't score VP but break ties. Whoever has the most village points wins. For your first game, a score in the 30 to 50 range is typical.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Management
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the elephant in the room, or rather, the ghost in the building. Don't panic about getting a ghost or two early on. A single ghost costs you a bit of reputation each round, but the cheap villagers it comes with give you more actions per round, which is often worth it. The key is knowing when to banish. If you have 2 or more ghosts, it's time to act. Banish when targeting a high-value opponent building for the best reward, and try to dump the ghost on someone who's leading in reputation.

walkthrough.title: Explore Early, Build Mid, Score Late
walkthrough.text: Your first couple of rounds should focus on exploration. Every cave card you collect is a potential outpost slot, and the encounter rewards fund your entire game. By round 3, shift toward building. You want houses generating goods and income before the final rounds. Rounds 5 through 7 are your scoring sprint. Harvest like crazy, fill your advancement track, and squeeze every VP out of your star buildings.

walkthrough.title: Dangerous Caves and Pearl Strategy
walkthrough.text: Dangerous caves are the high-risk, high-reward play. They need more lanterns to succeed, so bring 3 villagers and save a boat token for insurance. The pearl you earn on success is potentially worth 3 to 4 VP at endgame since it counts as your highest-value good. Two or three pearls can swing the entire game. But don't attempt dangerous caves with just 2 weak villagers. A failed exploration wastes your turn and injures your crew.

walkthrough.title: The Advancement Track
walkthrough.text: Goods variety is everything on the advancement track. Each new type of good opens a new slot, pushing your income higher and increasing the VP value of every good in later slots. If you have 5 different goods spread across 5 slots, your income is strong and each good in slots 4 and 5 might be worth 3 to 4 VP each. Compare that to stacking 5 of the same good in slot 1 for 0 VP each. Always prioritize getting a new good type over duplicating one you already have.

walkthrough.title: Building Synergies
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your buildings do together. A building with beds lets you recover villagers faster, meaning more actions per round. A building that grants boats gives you die mitigation for dangerous caves. Star buildings with conditions like 2 VP per cave card or 1 VP per potion can be huge if you build around them. Look at the available star houses early and plan your game around one or two of their conditions.

walkthrough.title: Villager Economy
walkthrough.text: More villagers means more actions per round, which is almost always better. Recruit early even if it means taking a ghost. A villager that gives you 2 or 3 extra actions over the course of the game is worth way more than the reputation hit from a ghost. But be smart about it. Quill villagers are especially valuable because they let you recruit AND banish. Having 2 quill villagers gives you flexibility to recruit one turn and banish the next.

walkthrough.title: Boat Token Optimization
walkthrough.text: Boats are probably the best improvement in Haunted over the original. They solve the biggest frustration of the explore action: bad dice rolls. Save your boats for dangerous cave explorations where the stakes are highest. A guaranteed 6 on one die can be the difference between 0 lanterns and 2 lanterns. In a pinch, trade a boat for a coin when you're 1 short of a critical building purchase. Don't hoard boats for the perfect moment that never comes.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Quick heads up, here are the traps new players fall into. First, exploring with only 2 villagers every time. Bring 3 when possible for much better lantern odds. Second, ignoring beds. Without villager recovery, you're dead in the water by round 5. Third, hoarding goods instead of placing them on the advancement track. Goods in your supply score nothing. Fourth, forgetting about the 7-round limit. This game is shorter than it feels. And fifth, banishing too early with just 1 ghost. Wait until you have 2 or more to get better value.

summary.title: Ghost Management
bullets: Tolerate 1 ghost early for the cheap villager workforce
Banish when you have 2+ ghosts
Target high-value opponent buildings for best banish rewards
Dump ghosts on reputation leaders to slow them down

entries.definition: Main action requiring 2 or more villagers. Draw a cave card, resolve an encounter from the encounter book, and gain rewards based on lantern totals. You keep the cave card regardless of success or failure.
Main action requiring a hammer villager. Pay the coin cost to purchase a house, key house, star house, or outpost card. Houses go above ground, outposts go on cave cards below ground.

Main action that exhausts one or more villagers. For each villager, take 1 good from a building or gain 1 coin. Combines the original game's harvest and labor actions. First harvester each round gains cider.
Main action requiring a quill villager. Pay the coin cost to hire a villager from the reputation board. Recruiting from the 3 cheapest slots adds a ghost to one of your buildings.

Main action requiring a quill villager and at least 1 ghost on your buildings. Remove all ghosts from your buildings, place 1 on an opponent's unghostted building, and gain a reward based on the targeted building type.

Wooden token placed on building cards. Each ghost lowers your reputation at end of round. Sources include cheap recruits, encounter results, and opponent banishing. Each building can hold only 1 ghost.
Token gained from encounters and building effects. As a free action, spend to auto-roll a 6 on one die during exploration or gain 1 coin. Provides crucial die mitigation.
Special token gained from successfully exploring dangerous caves. At endgame, each pearl scores VP equal to your highest-value good slot on the advancement track.
Token that can be spent during the rest phase to move 1 villager from injured to exhausted. Can be gained through encounters and some building effects.
Token gained by the first player to harvest each round. Spend during rest phase to move 1 villager from exhausted to ready. Replenished on the reputation board at end of each round.
Resource tokens in 8 types: fruit, fish, mushroom (common), rope, clay pot, paper (uncommon), ore, amethyst (rare). Placed on advancement track for income and VP, or harvested from buildings.
Building card placed above ground next to your starting house. Provides benefits like beds, goods slots, income, or special abilities. Costs coins and a hammer villager to build.
Building card placed on an empty cave card below ground. Must have an available cave card to build. Provides goods and special abilities. Represents underground construction.
Special building card with a key symbol. More powerful than regular houses with unique abilities. Only 4 available from the initial 9 shuffled; limited supply creates competition.
Special building card with a star symbol and bonus VP conditions. All 6 are displayed face up from the start. Conditions like '2 VP per potion' or '1 VP per cave card' can define your strategy.
Villager with a hammer icon. Required to perform the build action. Hammer-plus villagers pay 1 fewer coin when building.
Villager with a quill icon. Required to perform the recruit or banish action. Quill-plus villagers pay 1 fewer coin when recruiting. Especially versatile in Haunted due to the banish option.
Unique villagers with special abilities gained through encounters. Each has specific rules for how they behave differently from regular villagers.
The success currency for exploration. Each villager converts die rolls into lanterns based on their printed stats. Total lanterns must meet or exceed the explore number to succeed at an encounter.
During exploration, move a villager from the cave card to the injured area instead of exhausted to gain 1 extra lantern. Risky because injured villagers need potions or beds to recover.
Standard cave card drawn during exploration. Has moderate lantern requirements and standard rewards from encounters.
Cave card with distinct border requiring higher lantern totals. Gives bigger encounter rewards and a pearl token on success. Higher risk, higher reward.
Book of numbered paragraphs with branching narrative choices. Read by the player to your left during exploration. Each paragraph presents options with explore numbers and hidden rewards.

One of 40 unique cards gained as specific rewards from cave encounters. Provide immediate benefits, ongoing abilities, or end-game victory points. Cannot be purchased, only earned through exploration.
New to Haunted. Requires a quill villager and ghost presence. Clears all your ghosts, sends 1 to an opponent, and earns a reward. Strategic timing is key: wait for 2+ ghosts for better value.
The process of a building receiving a ghost token. Happens through cheap recruiting, encounter penalties, or opponent banishing. Haunted buildings lower reputation each round.
Row of circles at the bottom of your player board. Place goods left to right with no gaps, same types stack. Left number = VP per good at endgame, right number = current income level.
Track on the reputation board. Moving down (into the cave) is good, moving up is bad. Affected by encounters, ghost presence, and card effects. End-game bonus: 1st = 5 VP, 2nd = 3 VP, 3rd = 2 VP.
Coins collected at end of each round based on the rightmost occupied slot on your advancement track. More goods variety means higher income.
Section of your player board where available villagers wait. Only villagers in the ready area can be assigned to actions on your turn.
Section of your player board where used villagers go after performing actions. They need beds or cider to return to the ready area.
Section of your player board where exerted villagers go. They need potions to move to exhausted, then beds or cider to return to ready. Two steps to recovery.
End-of-round phase where players recover villagers. Spend potions (injured to exhausted), cider (exhausted to ready), and use building beds (one step per bed symbol).
Ryan Laukat's shared fantasy world where Above and Below: Haunted is set, along with the original Above and Below, Near and Far, and Now or Never. The setting is a haunted marshland this time.
